subset_tags.relevance_topic: Startup funding & venture,Personal savings & tax optimization

articles.title: New Tax Regime vs Old: Which One Saves You More Money in FY26? A Detailed Comparison
articles.content:
With the financial year drawing to a close, salaried individuals face the annual dilemma of choosing between the new and old income tax regimes. The decision has become more nuanced after the government sweetened the new regime in Budget 2025, raising the basic exemption limit and widening tax slabs.

Under the revised new regime, income up to Rs 4 lakh is fully exempt, and the effective tax-free threshold rises to Rs 12 lakh when the standard deduction of Rs 75,000 and the rebate under Section 87A are factored in. Tax rates range from 5% to 30% across six slabs, with the top rate kicking in only above Rs 24 lakh.

The old regime, meanwhile, retains the original slab structure with a basic exemption of Rs 2.5 lakh and a top rate of 30% above Rs 10 lakh. Its appeal lies in the suite of deductions available: Section 80C (up to Rs 1.5 lakh), Section 80D for health insurance premiums, HRA exemption, home loan interest under Section 24, and NPS contributions under Section 80CCD.

Tax advisor Archit Gupta, founder of ClearTax, says the break-even point has shifted. "If your total deductions and exemptions exceed roughly Rs 3.75 lakh per annum, the old regime may still save you more tax. Below that threshold, the new regime is almost certainly better," he explained.

For a salaried professional earning Rs 15 lakh annually, the new regime results in a tax liability of approximately Rs 1.35 lakh, compared to Rs 1.56 lakh under the old regime assuming standard deductions of Rs 2.5 lakh. However, someone earning the same amount but claiming deductions of Rs 4 lakh or more could save Rs 20,000-30,000 by staying with the old regime.

Experts advise taxpayers to run the numbers for their specific situation rather than relying on general guidance. Several free online calculators, including those offered by the Income Tax Department and major tax-filing platforms, can compare liabilities under both regimes in minutes.
articles.author: Babar Zaidi

articles.title: Startup Funding Rebounds Strongly: Indian Startups Raise $5.2 Billion in Q1 2026
articles.content:
Indian startups raised $5.2 billion across 287 deals in the first quarter of 2026, marking a 68% increase from the $3.1 billion raised in Q1 2025 and signalling a decisive end to the two-year funding winter that had gripped the ecosystem. The data, compiled by Tracxn, shows that both deal volumes and average ticket sizes have recovered to levels last seen in early 2022.

The recovery was led by a handful of large late-stage rounds. Quick-commerce major Zepto raised $850 million in a Series G round at a valuation of $8.5 billion, while enterprise SaaS firm Darwinbox secured $200 million from investors including Salesforce Ventures and TCV. Climate-tech startup Ather Energy completed a pre-IPO round of $180 million ahead of its planned public listing later this year.

Seed and early-stage funding also showed healthy growth, rising 34% year-on-year to $780 million across 198 deals. Investors are particularly enthusiastic about startups building applications on top of generative AI infrastructure. AI-focused startups accounted for nearly 22% of all deals in the quarter, up from 11% a year ago.

"The market has recalibrated after the excesses of 2021-22," said Rajan Anandan, managing director of Peak XV Partners (formerly Sequoia India). "Founders are more disciplined about unit economics, and investors are more rigorous about due diligence. This is a healthier funding environment."

The IPO pipeline has also provided a significant confidence boost. Successful listings by Swiggy, FirstCry and Ola Electric in 2025 demonstrated that public market exits are viable for Indian startups, encouraging late-stage investors to deploy capital.

Sector-wise, fintech continued to attract the most funding at $1.4 billion, followed by enterprise tech at $980 million, consumer internet at $870 million and health-tech at $520 million. Geographically, Bengaluru-based startups accounted for 42% of total funding, followed by Delhi-NCR at 28% and Mumbai at 18%.

However, challenges remain. Valuations for mid-stage companies remain 20-30% below their 2021 peaks, and profitability expectations have become non-negotiable for growth-stage companies seeking new rounds.
articles.author: Samidha Sharma

articles.title: NPS Tax Benefits Explained: How the National Pension System Can Save You Up to Rs 2 Lakh in Taxes
articles.content:
The National Pension System remains one of the most tax-efficient retirement savings instruments available to Indian taxpayers, yet it is significantly underutilised compared to traditional options like PPF and ELSS. With the financial year ending in less than a week, here is a clear breakdown of how NPS can help you save up to Rs 2 lakh in taxes.

The primary tax benefit comes under Section 80CCD(1), which allows you to claim a deduction of up to 10% of your salary (basic plus dearness allowance) for NPS contributions. This deduction falls within the overall Rs 1.5 lakh limit of Section 80C, which means it competes with other popular instruments like PPF, ELSS and life insurance premiums.

The real advantage, however, lies in Section 80CCD(1B), which provides an additional deduction of up to Rs 50,000 exclusively for NPS contributions. This is over and above the Rs 1.5 lakh limit of Section 80C, giving NPS subscribers a unique tax-saving advantage that no other instrument offers.

For salaried individuals, there is a third benefit under Section 80CCD(2): employer contributions to NPS of up to 14% of salary (for central government employees) or 10% (for others) are deductible from taxable income without any upper cap. This effectively means that for someone with a basic salary of Rs 10 lakh, employer NPS contributions of up to Rs 1 lakh are fully tax-free.

Combining all three provisions, a taxpayer in the highest 30% tax bracket can save approximately Rs 62,400 in taxes annually, or over Rs 2 lakh if employer contributions are factored in.

It is important to note that these benefits are available only under the old tax regime. Under the new regime, only the employer contribution under Section 80CCD(2) remains available as a deduction.

On the investment side, NPS offers a choice of equity, corporate bonds and government securities across multiple fund managers. Aggressive allocation NPS portfolios have delivered annualised returns of 12-14% over the past decade, comfortably outpacing inflation.

The partial withdrawal rules have also been liberalised. Subscribers can withdraw up to 25% of their own contributions after three years for specified purposes including children's education, home purchase or medical treatment. At retirement, 60% of the corpus can be withdrawn tax-free, while the remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity.
articles.author: Renu Yadav

articles.title: Bond Yields Drop Sharply After RBI Rate Cut; 10-Year G-Sec Falls to 14-Month Low of 6.72%
articles.content:
Government bond prices surged and yields fell sharply on Thursday after the Reserve Bank of India delivered a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the repo rate, bringing it down to 6.0%. The benchmark 10-year government security yield dropped 8 basis points to close at 6.72%, its lowest level since January 2025.

The rally was broad-based across the yield curve, with short-term securities outperforming as traders priced in the possibility of further rate cuts in the coming months. The 5-year government bond yield fell 11 basis points to 6.48%, while the 2-year yield dropped 14 basis points to 6.21%.

The RBI's decision to maintain an accommodative stance and its upward revision of GDP growth signalled that the central bank sees room for additional monetary easing without stoking inflation. Interest rate futures are now pricing in a 70% probability of another 25 basis point cut at the June policy meeting.

For corporate treasuries and chief financial officers managing large debt portfolios, the falling yield environment presents both opportunities and considerations. Companies with floating-rate borrowings will see an immediate reduction in interest costs, while those looking to issue fresh bonds can lock in historically attractive rates.

"We are advising our corporate clients to consider prefunding their FY27 borrowing requirements now, given the favourable rate environment," said Ajay Manglunia, managing director and head of institutional fixed income at JM Financial. "The window for sub-7% corporate bond issuance may not remain open indefinitely."

Mutual funds focused on gilt and long-duration strategies were among the day's biggest winners. The ICICI Prudential Long Term Bond Fund gained 0.42% in a single session, while the SBI Magnum Gilt Fund rose 0.38%.

Foreign portfolio investors have been steady buyers of Indian government bonds since the JPMorgan index inclusion in June 2024, with cumulative inflows of $32 billion into the designated fully accessible route securities.
articles.author: Saikat Das
